memes.label: Adversity
memes.description: External hardship that tests moral character in Charlotte Temple — presented by Rowson as a refining fire that distinguishes genuine virtue from mere good fortune.

memes.label: Affection
memes.description: A foundational emotional bond in Rowson's moral universe — the natural, virtuous attachment between parents and children, husbands and wives, and genuine lovers, whose absence or betrayal signals moral decay.

memes.label: Anger
memes.description: A morally ambivalent emotion in Charlotte Temple — righteous anger can defend virtue and enforce moral order, while uncontrolled anger (like Montraville's) drives destruction and regret.

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Physical attractiveness as both gift and danger in Charlotte Temple (1791) — a quality that attracts admiration and desire but also renders its possessor vulnerable to seduction, making beauty the novel's most ambivalent attribute.

memes.label: Benevolence
memes.description: Active goodwill toward others, celebrated in Charlotte Temple as the highest social virtue — the quality that binds community, relieves suffering, and stands as the moral opposite of the cruelty that destroys the novel's heroine.

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: The Christian virtue of compassionate giving, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as both a moral obligation and a social reality — the force that should rescue the fallen but too often fails them, exposing the gap between Christian ideals and social practice.

memes.label: Comfort
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple, comfort represents both material well-being and emotional solace — the novel charts Charlotte's progressive loss of both as she falls from her parents' comfortable home into poverty, and presents genuine comfort as available only through virtue and religion.

memes.label: Compassion
memes.description: The capacity to feel for and be moved by the suffering of others — in Rowson's sentimental novel, the redemptive emotional response that distinguishes virtuous characters from the callous world, and the reader's own proper affective stance toward the fallen heroine.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concepts and themes in Charlotte Temple, from virtue and vice to social institutions and emotions.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The internal moral monitor in Charlotte Temple (1791), presented both as Montraville's belated accuser and as the reader's protective guide — the voice that distinguishes the fallen from those Providence has preserved.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), courage appears primarily as a domestic and moral virtue — the fortitude to endure suffering, to confront painful truths, and to resist social pressure — rather than as martial or public heroism.

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: The infliction of suffering upon the vulnerable, presented in Charlotte Temple as the defining moral failure of its male antagonists and the narrative's most potent indictment of seduction culture.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple, death is the novel's ultimate moral arena — the deathbed is where truth is told, forgiveness is granted, and the full consequences of seduction are reckoned. Rowson presents death as both punishment and release, the final scene in her didactic drama.

memes.label: Deceit
memes.description: The moral failure of misrepresentation and betrayal, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a pervasive threat to vulnerable innocence — a quality attributed both to seducers' schemes and to the self-deceptions of the heart.

memes.label: Despair
memes.description: The dissolution of hope, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as the terminal emotional state of the fallen — the point at which suffering eclipses the capacity to imagine recovery, and the soul confronts its own destruction.

memes.label: Devotion
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), outward religious observance that may or may not reflect inner virtue — genuine piety among the Chichester schoolgirls contrasts with the ironic "devotions" paid to the coquettish La Rue.

memes.label: Distress
memes.description: The visible face of suffering in Charlotte Temple — distress is the emotional and physical state that signals moral crisis, evokes sympathy, and calls forth the novel's central virtue of compassion toward the afflicted.

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The moral obligation to fulfill one's responsibilities to parents, religion, and social order — a binding force that Rowson positions as the active component of virtue and the safeguard against seduction.

memes.label: Education
memes.description: A central concern in Charlotte Temple (1791), where Rowson critiques both the inadequacy of female education and the dangers of a "mistaken education" that leaves young women vulnerable to seduction.

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), experience is a double-edged concept: the painful knowledge gained through suffering and error, contrasted with the dangerous inexperience of youth that leaves the innocent vulnerable to seduction and deception.

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: The providential force that shapes the destinies of characters in Charlotte Temple, serving as both a narrative framework for tragedy and a vehicle for moral instruction in Rowson's sentimental universe.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: A pervasive emotional state in Charlotte Temple — fear of parental disapproval, fear of ruin, and fear of abandonment — that Rowson uses both as a warning signal of moral danger and as a consequence of moral transgression.

memes.label: Filial Piety
memes.description: The moral obligation of children to honour, obey, and care for their parents — in Rowson's novel, filial piety is the foundational virtue whose violation triggers the central tragedy and whose enduring power makes possible the novel's redemptive conclusion.

memes.label: Folly
memes.description: The failure of prudence and judgment that renders young women vulnerable to seduction — in Rowson's moral vocabulary, folly is not mere foolishness but a morally consequential lapse in reason and self-governance that opens the path to vice and ruin.

memes.label: Forgiveness
memes.description: The moral and emotional act of pardoning wrongdoing, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a Christian virtue and the ultimate mechanism of familial restoration — though one that, tragically, arrives too late to save the novel's heroine.

memes.label: Friendship
memes.description: A bond of mutual affection and moral obligation that, in Rowson's novel, appears in both its genuine form — as a source of support and moral guidance — and its counterfeit, where professions of friendship mask predatory designs upon the innocent.

memes.label: Gratitude
memes.description: The moral obligation to acknowledge and reciprocate kindness, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a fundamental social bond whose violation — ingratitude — constitutes one of the gravest moral failures in Rowson's sentimental universe.

memes.label: Grief
memes.description: The overwhelming sorrow of loss, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as both a natural human response and a narrative mechanism through which Rowson stages the emotional costs of seduction for victims and families alike.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: The moral and psychological burden of wrongdoing, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as both an internal affliction and a social marker — the seduced woman's inescapable companion from the moment of her fall.

memes.label: Independence
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), independence denotes the economic and moral condition of being free from obligation to others — a state Rowson presents as essential to virtuous marriage, personal dignity, and resistance to the manipulations of the predatory.

memes.label: Innocence
memes.description: The state of moral and sexual purity that the sentimental novel positions as a young woman's natural condition and most precious possession — a tranquillity that, once lost through seduction, can never be fully restored in worldly terms.

memes.label: Kindness
memes.description: The active practice of gentleness and generosity toward others, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a counterforce to cruelty — the quality that distinguishes characters who alleviate suffering from those who compound it.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: The state of freedom from constraint, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) in both its physical sense (freedom from imprisonment) and its moral sense — where liberty from parental authority becomes the instrument of ruin rather than a blessing.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple, love appears in two contrasting forms — the sanctioned mutual affection of virtuous marriage exemplified by the Temples, and the dangerous unsanctioned passion that, severed from parental approbation and reason, becomes the engine of seduction and ruin.

memes.label: Lucy Temple
memes.description: Charlotte's devoted younger sister, whose steady filial affection and quiet virtue provide a living contrast to Charlotte's fall — Lucy remains at home, sustains her grieving parents, and embodies the domestic stability that Charlotte forfeited.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: A social institution that, in Charlotte Temple (1791), stands as the boundary between virtue and ruin — promised as a reward, weaponized as a deception, and ultimately denied to the novel's heroine, whose tragedy is defined by her exclusion from its protections.

memes.label: Melancholy
memes.description: The pervasive emotional state that marks both the novel's narrative voice and its heroine's decline — melancholy in Rowson's treatment is the affective register of moral loss, the felt consequence of folly, and the emotional precondition of penitence.

memes.label: Misfortune
memes.description: A central thematic concept in Charlotte Temple encompassing financial ruin, personal catastrophe, and the loss of virtue — presented by Rowson as both a moral test and, when survived with integrity, a refiners of character.

memes.label: Modesty
memes.description: The feminine virtue of restrained self-presentation, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as both a natural endowment and a social discipline — the quality that protects female innocence from the predatory gaze and whose absence signals moral decay.

memes.label: Mrs. Temple
memes.description: Charlotte's devoted mother — a model of maternal virtue, tenderness, and ultimately forgiveness in Rowson's moral universe. Her anguish at Charlotte's fall and her redemptive presence at Charlotte's deathbed embody the novel's central themes of parental love and Christian mercy.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The innate constitution of human beings, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a moral framework that both explains vulnerability to passion and grounds the possibility of virtue — "nature, all-powerful nature" as the force that binds parents to children and the fallen to their conscience.

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: The powerful force of unregulated emotion and desire that, in Rowson's moral universe, overthrows reason, undermines virtue, and drives the central action of seduction — passion is the engine of catastrophe for both the seducer and the seduced.

memes.label: Penitence
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), penitence is the redemptive virtue that allies the fallen sufferer with moral worth — the tear of genuine remorse that blots offences "from the book of fate" and restores the penitent to virtue's fold.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all characters in Charlotte Temple, sorted alphabetically with quality indicators.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Index of all places in Charlotte Temple—England, America, and the transitional spaces that define the novel's geography of virtue and ruin.

memes.label: Poverty
memes.description: The state of material deprivation, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as the inevitable terminus of the fallen woman's trajectory — "guilt, poverty, and disease" closing the dreadful scene of seduction's consequences.

memes.label: "Preface"
memes.description: "The author's prefatory address to the young and thoughtless of the fair sex, explaining the moral purpose of the tale."

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: A double-edged moral force in Charlotte Temple (1791) — the aristocratic family pride that traps individuals in mercenary marriages, and the personal pride that fuels revenge and prevents forgiveness.

memes.label: Prudence
memes.description: The virtue of exercising caution, forethought, and sound moral judgment — in Rowson's sentimental novel, a young woman's essential safeguard against seduction and ruin, and the quality whose absence drives the novel's tragic plot.

memes.label: Regret
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), regret is the retrospective pain of moral error — a salvific emotion that signals the sinner's return to virtue, and the mechanism through which Rowson's villains are judged and her heroines redeemed.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: The Christian moral framework that underpins Charlotte Temple (1791), invoked by Rowson as the ultimate authority against seduction, suicide, and despair — a source of consolation for the suffering and a standard by which all characters are judged.

memes.label: Remorse
memes.description: The acute psychological anguish of conscience, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as the inescapable emotional consequence of moral transgression — the bed of thorns upon which the fallen must lie.

memes.label: Repentance
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple, repentance is the mechanism by which a fallen individual can achieve spiritual restoration — "nearly allied to virtue" (025-045), it cannot undo the worldly consequences of sin but can restore the soul to divine favour through sincere sorrow and amendment of life, as enacted in Charlotte's deathbed confession.

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: A social and moral asset in the world of Charlotte Temple (1791), where a woman's reputation is both her most precious possession and her most fragile defence — easily imperilled by imprudence and, once lost, nearly impossible to recover.

memes.label: Resentment
memes.description: A destructive emotion in Charlotte Temple (1791) weaponised by Belcour against Charlotte and briefly felt by Montraville himself — the bitter feeling that poisons relationships and accelerates the novel's tragedy.

memes.label: Ruin
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple, ruin denotes the catastrophic loss of virtue, reputation, and social standing that follows a young woman's seduction — characterized by Charlotte herself as a "dark abyss" (012-044) of shame and remorse from which no worldly recovery is possible, though spiritual restoration through penitence remains open.

memes.label: Seduction
memes.description: The act of luring a virtuous young woman from the path of rectitude through flattery, false promises, and emotional manipulation — portrayed in Charlotte Temple as a predatory cruelty that the narrator denounces as monstrous and that drives the novel's central tragedy.

memes.label: Sensibility
memes.description: The capacity for refined and acute feeling — both a moral virtue essential to compassion and a dangerous vulnerability that, unregulated by reason and principle, renders young women susceptible to seduction and ruin in Rowson's moral universe.

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: The social emotion of public disgrace and self-reproach, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as the fallen woman's inescapable companion — the blush that marks her moral fall and the burden that bows her "to the earth."

memes.label: Sorrow
memes.description: In Charlotte Temple (1791), sorrow is the emotional register of the sentimental novel itself — the suffering that teaches moral lessons, the grief that attests to virtue, and the sympathetic pain that binds reader to character in Rowson's didactic project.

memes.label: Temptation
memes.description: The moral danger that besets young women in Charlotte Temple (1791), figured both as the seducer's "snares" that trap the unprotected female and as the trial that parental prayer and religious precept are meant to guard against.

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: The quality of moral excellence and uprightness that serves as the central moral standard in Charlotte Temple — a standard the heroine is educated to value but tragically fails to uphold under the pressure of seduction and circumstance.

memes.label: Weakness
memes.description: A gendered moral concept in Charlotte Temple — weakness is the ascribed vulnerability of women to seduction, but Rowson complicates this by distinguishing between pardonable physical frailty and culpable moral weakness, locating true strength in virtue and parental firmness.

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: Material abundance as both a moral resource and a moral danger, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a blessing that enables charity but also fosters the indulgence that makes the young vulnerable to seduction.

memes.label: Youth
memes.description: The stage of life marked by innocence, inexperience, and vulnerability, presented in Charlotte Temple (1791) as a period of acute moral danger — when "the mind of youth eagerly catches at promised pleasure" without recognizing the snares beneath.
